sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 444, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (32,47), 4px. 'c' frames at (28,16) and (28,32). Player moved too far right. Need to approach 'c' frame at (28,16) from the right.
- Plan: Move LEFT 7, UP 4 to approach 'c' frame at (28,16).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 443 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 6.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so the player moved LEFT 2 times (only 2 actions executed out of 7 LEFT + 4 UP). The player is now at (32, 36), and the 'N' object is at (32, 37).
Looking at the ascii:
Row 28: wwwwccccwwwwwwwwbbbbccccbbbbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 29: wwwwcbbcwwwwwwwwbgggcbbcgggbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 30: wwwwcbbcwwwwwwwwbgggcbbcgggbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 31: wwwwccccwwwwwwwwbbbbccccbbbbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 32: wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwWNNNwwwwwwwwwww
The player is at (32, 36), which is below the 'c' frame at (28, 32). The 'c' frame at (28, 32) is the one that was previously a 'G' frame.
Wait, the player is at (32, 36), and the 'c' frame is at (28, 32). The player is 4 rows below and 4 columns to the right of the 'c' frame.
Let me think about this. The player is at (32, 36), and the 'c' frame is at (28, 32). If I move UP 4 times, the player will be at (28, 36), which is to the right of the 'c' frame. Then the 'c' frame might transform to 'G'.
But wait, the 'c' frame at (28, 32) is the one that was previously a 'G' frame. When the player was close to it, it transformed to 'G'. When the player moved away, it transformed back to 'c'.
So if I move the player close to the 'c' frame at (28, 32), it should transform to 'G'. Then I can press SPACE to toggle to 16px and absorb it.
Let me try moving UP 4 times to reach (28, 36), which is adjacent to the right side of the 'c' frame at (28, 32).
But wait, the 'c' frame at (28, 32) is at rows 28-31, cols 32-35. The player at (28, 36) would be adjacent to the right side of the frame (col 36 is next to col 35).
Actually, looking at the ascii:
Row 28: wwwwccccwwwwwwwwbbbbccccbbbbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 29: wwwwcbbcwwwwwwwwbgggcbbcgggbwwwwwwwwwww
The 'c' frame at (28, 32) is at cols 32-35 (cccc). The 'b' frame parts are at cols 28-31 (bbbb) and cols 36-39 (bbbb).
So the 'c' frame at (28, 32) is between the two 'b' frame parts. The player at (32, 36) is below the right 'b' frame part.
If I move UP 4 times, the player will be at (28, 36), which is at the same row as the 'c' frame, but at col 36, which is the right 'b' frame part. This might cause the player to move into the 'b' frame part.
Let me think about this more carefully. The player is at (32, 36). The 'c' frame is at (28, 32), occupying rows 28-31, cols 32-35. The right 'b' frame part is at (28, 36), occupying rows 28-31, cols 36-39.
If I move UP 4 times, the player will move from (32, 36) to (28, 36), which is the top-left corner of the right 'b' frame part. This might cause the player to move into the 'b' frame part, splitting it or transforming it.
Actually, I think the issue is that the player is moving too fast. The player moved LEFT 2 times from (32, 47) to (32, 36), which is 11 columns in 2 actions. That's about 5.5 columns per action.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of moving the player around, let me try to approach the 'c' frame at (28, 16) by moving LEFT and UP more carefully.
The player is at (32, 36). The 'c' frame at (28, 16) is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. To approach it from the right, I need to reach (28, 20).
From (32, 36) to (28, 20), I need to move LEFT 16 columns and UP 4 rows. If the player moves about 5-6 columns per LEFT action, then 16 / 5 ≈ 3 LEFT actions. Let me try LEFT 3, UP 4.
But I'm not confident about the movement speed. Let me just try LEFT 3, UP 4 and see where the player ends up.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. The player has absorbed 2 'G' frames so far (the first one at the beginning, and the middle one from the split 'b' frame). The remaining frames are:
- c: pos=(24,44), pixels=12 (top-right)
- c: pos=(28,16), pixels=12 (left)
- c: pos=(28,32), pixels=12 (middle - was 'G' frame)
- b: pos=(28,28), pixels=10 (left split 'b' part)
- b: pos=(28,36), pixels=10 (right split 'b' part)
The 'c' frames are the original frames that haven't been absorbed yet. The 'b' frame parts are the split parts from the middle frame.
Maybe the goal is to absorb all 3 original 'c' frames. The 'b' frame parts might be a distraction or might need to be dealt with separately.
Let me focus on absorbing the 'c' frame at (28, 32) first, since it's the closest to the player. The player is at (32, 36), and the 'c' frame is at (28, 32).
If I move UP 4 times, the player will be at (28, 36), which is adjacent to the right side of the 'c' frame. But the player might also be adjacent to the right 'b' frame part.
Let me try moving UP 4 times and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (32,36), 4px. 'c' frame at (28,32) is closest. Player below-right of it.
Plan: Move UP 4 to approach 'c' frame at (28,32) from the right side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Move UP 4 to approach 'c' frame at (28,32)
result = action(['UP']*4)
print(f"Result: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nKey objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 50:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(r for r,c in b)
        c_min = min(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pos=({r_min},{c_min}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAscii rows 24-40, cols 12-50:")
for r in range(24, 41):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][12:51]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
